questions.text: I sense when a person is choosing to sit in a specific seat because of who is already sitting where.
I trust the group's consensus more than my own individual judgment.
I find that rewarding myself after completing a personal goal reinforces my discipline.
I adjust course continuously rather than waiting for a formal review session.
I am the person others call when something needs to get decided.
I feel that the moral concern generated by witnessing suffering is the force that keeps my ethical commitments grounded in reality.
I tend to set the pace in group work rather than follow it.
I experience joy as a deep, settled feeling rather than an effervescent high.
I believe that remaining emotionally uninvolved during others' crises is a legitimate and often necessary approach to maximizing positive impact.
I keep a curated collection of reference materials organized by topic.
I notice when someone is testing the waters with a comment before committing to an opinion.
I study the instructions for new software before using it.
I feel that securing the best possible outcome for myself in a negotiation is simply smart.
I hold the view that my nervous system operates with a kind of built-in buffer zone.
I find that my impulse when I learn something new is to teach it to someone who could benefit.
I prefer to keep my options open rather than lock myself into a specific course of action.
My nervous system seems to amplify everything — the good is great, the bad is terrible, and the neutral is rare.
I feel that the future is something you design through deliberate action in the present.
I hold the view that suffering which is visible to me and within my capacity to address constitutes a moral obligation, not merely an opportunity.
I hold the view that the person who adapts the fastest is the one who succeeds.
I find it difficult to overrule suggestions, even when they do not align with my own thinking.
I hold the view that healthy self-interest is the bedrock of a productive life.
I find that emotional distance from a crisis situation allows me to identify the highest-impact actions more accurately.
I hold the view that a life lived for others is the most meaningful kind of life.
I believe that hosting others in my home, even at personal inconvenience, is an essential practice.
I experience the end of a meaningful period — a trip, a project, a visit — with quiet acceptance rather than anguish.
I hold the view that the moral concern I feel when confronted with suffering is the experience that gives my sense of justice its emotional grounding and its motivational force.
I enjoy being the public face of a group effort.
I notice that my digestive system reacts strongly to emotional stress — nausea, cramping, loss of appetite.
I detect when someone emphasizes a particular word to send a message that only certain listeners will catch.
I would rather check in with others than risk overstepping in a crisis.
I focus on the literal meaning of words rather than the context surrounding them.
I believe that professionals who maintain their analytical composure during tragic events are providing a service that emotional responders cannot.
I tend to be oblivious to the backstory between people I meet.
My emotional responses are so rapid that they sometimes surprise even me.
I often miss hints and wish people would just be direct with me.
